messages.author: drewzh
messages.text: I so miss being able to send/receive packets over a protocal such as mqtt. Currently I'm bridging the gap by using the home assistant integration, but it requires a dedicated node on the Unraid server where I run home assistant (which is actually proving to be flakey for some reason) and I'd love to be able to just control the attic node directly over TCP.

messages.author: warmax356
messages.text: For urban networks i can see the appeal of lora + internet: for example if i have a node (base stations) with mqtt those that may be out of lora range but on wifi say at work (big offices/industrial factories) this may be used as an alternative to remain connected, client uses wifi for mqtt broker and still get there pings basestations without other lora bases in line of sight ( like urban areas and hilly tarrain) may be connected to the network for broader coverage and not just act as a standalone for where it is. its one of the things i am looking into for the maltese islands at the moment as meshcore and meshtastic are very scarce here and planning a community deployment where mqtt backhaul may be advantageous to the mesh especially in large complexes where the mesh may not overlap so easily with large and thick concrete/limestone/reinforced walls, besides that there is alot of signal noise form a ton of devices
messages.content_html:
For urban networks i can see the appeal of lora + internet: for example if i have a node (base stations) with mqtt those that may be out of lora range but on wifi say at work (big offices/industrial factories) this may be used as an alternative to remain connected,
  • client uses wifi for mqtt broker and still get there pings
  • basestations without other lora bases in line of sight ( like urban areas and hilly tarrain) may be connected to the network for broader coverage and not just act as a standalone for where it is.
its one of the things i am looking into for the maltese islands at the moment as meshcore and meshtastic are very scarce here and planning a community deployment where mqtt backhaul may be advantageous to the mesh especially in large complexes where the mesh may not overlap so easily with large and thick concrete/limestone/reinforced walls, besides that there is alot of signal noise form a ton of devices

messages.author: Dadud
messages.text: In my state, mesh coverage outside the capitol is non-existent currently. Employing something to link repeaters via MQTT together to bridge coverage gaps, and allow companion radio nodes to message any room servers or other radios on the other side of the bridge, atleast until more people get onboard would be handy. Would also help Rural users like myself keep interested enough to continue playing with the nodes. Currently with mt I have nobody local besides a single node about an hour away that I see when a plane flies over. Would help keep interest in Lora mesh, and bring new people in. Whatever happens please don't zero hop MQTT like the green nodes do. It was very disappointing to me when they did that
messages.content_html: In my state, mesh coverage outside the capitol is non-existent currently. Would also help Rural users like myself keep interested enough to continue playing with the nodes. Currently with mt I have nobody local besides a single node about an hour away that I see when a plane flies over. Would help keep interest in Lora mesh, and bring new people in. Whatever happens please don't zero hop MQTT like the green nodes do. It was very disappointing to me when they did that

messages.author: Dadud
messages.text: I would also not run a default meshcore MQTT server, I would leave it in up to state/county/country wide groups to run their own. And not disable MQTT reporting by default in firmware for anything besides location
messages.content_html: I would also not run a default meshcore MQTT server, I would leave it in up to state/county/country wide groups to run their own. And not disable MQTT reporting by default in firmware for anything besides location

messages.author: trisweb
messages.text: I strongly oppose MeshCore having the ability to connect any nodes to the internet as a messaging bridge (telemetry and stats is fine, but we can already do that). It is entirely against the point of the project, and furthermore, since it’s a mesh and RF airtime is a scarce resource, I can’t “opt-out” of this behavior because it impacts me even if I don’t personally turn it on. Therefore it must be impossible by design (as much as possible) for any nodes to route messages from the internet to the mesh.
messages.content_html: I strongly oppose MeshCore having the ability to connect any nodes to the internet as a messaging bridge (telemetry and stats is fine, but we can already do that). It is entirely against the point of the project, and furthermore, since it’s a mesh and RF airtime is a scarce resource, I can’t “opt-out” of this behavior because it impacts me even if I don’t personally turn it on. Therefore it must be impossible by design (as much as possible) for any nodes to route messages from the internet to the mesh.

messages.author: Beanow
messages.text: Nothing is stopping anyone from implementing such an internet backhaul. With the protocol / fw being MIT especially. However as you can tell from the reactions, people really consider this an anti-feature. Personally, I do see a use-case for it. Because being in a larger mesh where you can actually find a community sounds more fun than being in a 5 person early adopters bubble in your town. If I were to deploy something like this, with the goal of making it "more fun" to adopt in new locations. I'd choose a very rough brownout schedule. For example run this backhaul only 3 days per week.
messages.content_html: Nothing is stopping anyone from implementing such an internet backhaul. With the protocol / fw being MIT especially. However as you can tell from the reactions, people really consider this an anti-feature. Personally, I do see a use-case for it. Because being in a larger mesh where you can actually find a community sounds more fun than being in a 5 person early adopters bubble in your town. If I were to deploy something like this, with the goal of making it "more fun" to adopt in new locations. I'd choose a very rough brownout schedule. For example run this backhaul only 3 days per week.

messages.author: Beanow
messages.text: I agree partially. Right now we don't have enough information to say what the resilience is like anyway. There's no complete information about repeater power supply situations. There's no organised large scale scenario testing. Etc. etc. It'll require more data, more research, more analysis tools, more tests.
messages.content_html: I agree partially. Right now we don't have enough information to say what the resilience is like anyway. There's no complete information about repeater power supply situations. There's no organised large scale scenario testing. Etc. etc. It'll require more data, more research, more analysis tools, more tests.

messages.author: trisweb
messages.text: I think this is black & white. If you make the mesh fun using the internet, it discourages building an actual mesh. It is the right incentive to not have fun happen until you build a real mesh. I personally believe this is why real meshes are being built out: because people want to join in, and then only way to join in is to build a mesh that works. Because of this, we get a mesh that works. We should not just use caution: we should protect this by design.
messages.content_html: I think this is black & white. If you make the mesh fun using the internet, it discourages building an actual mesh. It is the right incentive to not have fun happen until you build a real mesh. I personally believe this is why real meshes are being built out: because people want to join in, and then only way to join in is to build a mesh that works. Because of this, we get a mesh that works. We should not just use caution: we should protect this by design.
messages.reactions_html: 6

messages.author: Beanow
messages.text: I think it's not black&white, it's speculation whether this is useful tech or not. Imagine this situation. Huge industrial building. An array of 4 yagi repeaters that provide awesome coverage in their respective directions. But the hops between them are terribly unreliable. Option 1, put several small omni's in between and hope that works. Option 2, solar + grid + aggregate redundant power, LAN cables and a router program on a computer in between. No internet involved. That's also a TCP/IP backhaul, which improves reliability and frees up the airtime that the small omnis would have taken. (edited)
messages.content_html: I think it's not black&white, it's speculation whether this is useful tech or not. Imagine this situation. Huge industrial building. An array of 4 yagi repeaters that provide awesome coverage in their respective directions. But the hops between them are terribly unreliable. Option 1, put several small omni's in between and hope that works. Option 2, solar + grid + aggregate redundant power, LAN cables and a router program on a computer in between. No internet involved. That's also a TCP/IP backhaul, which improves reliability and frees up the airtime that the small omnis would have taken. (edited)

messages.author: trisweb
messages.text: Nothing stops you, but that kind of thing improves the reliability of the mesh in the real world (without using the public internet). You’re right it’s not absolute, transports that aren’t LoRa are arguably fine, but I think where it starts to do more harm than good is by using the Internet and bridging using worldwide infrastructure that already exists and that we want to be independent of. If we add a dependency on something that we’re specifically trying to build independence from, that’s the line. Lack of control over what people do is inherent to complex social systems, but that is why design is so important. We don’t have control, but we have influence in the design of the protocol, what it allows and encourages, and what it makes difficult and discourages. And this is reinforced and reciprocated in social ways too with values, norms, and incentives. We value the design of the mesh and its incentives, and we reward following those values. It’s all one system and that’s why I think these discussions and principles are important to discuss and align on (so thank you!)
messages.content_html: Nothing stops you, but that kind of thing improves the reliability of the mesh in the real world (without using the public internet). You’re right it’s not absolute, transports that aren’t LoRa are arguably fine, but I think where it starts to do more harm than good is by using the Internet and bridging using worldwide infrastructure that already exists and that we want to be independent of. If we add a dependency on something that we’re specifically trying to build independence from, that’s the line. Lack of control over what people do is inherent to complex social systems, but that is why design is so important. We don’t have control, but we have influence in the design of the protocol, what it allows and encourages, and what it makes difficult and discourages. And this is reinforced and reciprocated in social ways too with values, norms, and incentives. We value the design of the mesh and its incentives, and we reward following those values. It’s all one system and that’s why I think these discussions and principles are important to discuss and align on (so thank you!)

messages.author: Signalwar
messages.text: Why it's good : Cheap and remote setups (with only 1 wifi connected node you can make a local mesh connected to the main mesh without needing premission and 1000$) Travelling (while on holiday you can setup one node at your hotel and reach anywhere in the world even in places without connection the the mesh.) Islands (even out to sea you can reach the mainland) Car canvoy (wifi connect one car on the canvoy before driving and enjoy connection to the mesh even in the remotest of roads) Boating (put nodes on the top of the boat like a sail and then have a satalite antenna for wifi like a spacex mini and enjoy connection to the main mesh while not needed wifi for all boats) Why not : It can weaken long range connections because people might rely on wifi (it makes the network stronger and more intra connected 99.9999999% of the time but maybe weakens it 0.0000001 of the time. If all sim towers land lines and wifi connections go down for every single wifi connected node in which is extreme you would need a total blackout that is unseen in modern times . ) just as my strongest argument against that people would have these nodes like a t beam supreme connected without a battery or power bank in their homes connected to their wifi so they would go down even if they didn't have a mqtt bridge anf become useless to the network. No one is installing a solar wifi node if they aren't crazy (edited)
messages.content_html:
Why it's good :

Cheap and remote setups (with only 1 wifi connected node you can make a local mesh connected to the main mesh without needing premission and 1000$) Travelling (while on holiday you can setup one node at your hotel and reach anywhere in the world even in places without connection the the mesh.) Islands (even out to sea you can reach the mainland) Car canvoy (wifi connect one car on the canvoy before driving and enjoy connection to the mesh even in the remotest of roads) Boating (put nodes on the top of the boat like a sail and then have a satalite antenna for wifi like a spacex mini and enjoy connection to the main mesh while not needed wifi for all boats)

Why not :

It can weaken long range connections because people might rely on wifi (it makes the network stronger and more intra connected 99.9999999% of the time but maybe weakens it 0.0000001 of the time. If all sim towers land lines and wifi connections go down for every single wifi connected node in which is extreme you would need a total blackout that is unseen in modern times .)

just as my strongest argument against that

people would have these nodes like a t beam supreme connected without a battery or power bank in their homes connected to their wifi so they would go down even if they didn't have a mqtt bridge anf become useless to the network. No one is installing a solar wifi node if they aren't crazy (edited)

messages.author: trisweb
messages.text: MeshCore is for building mesh networks that are independent and RF linked over LoRa. There are many other ways to create networks over the internet or even over HF radio for other uses; you don’t have to use MeshCore. This is kind of like saying a hammer should have a slot on the end for turning screws, because you don’t need to hammer nails, you need to screw in screws. It’s okay to just use a screwdriver, you don’t need to use the hammer. (edited)
messages.content_html: MeshCore is for building mesh networks that are independent and RF linked over LoRa. There are many other ways to create networks over the internet or even over HF radio for other uses; you don’t have to use MeshCore. This is kind of like saying a hammer should have a slot on the end for turning screws, because you don’t need to hammer nails, you need to screw in screws. It’s okay to just use a screwdriver, you don’t need to use the hammer. (edited)

messages.author: [PNW] HerculesMulligan
messages.text: It does take a certain amount of (unearned) confidence to still have the "I'm new here, say hi!" and then argue with people who have successfully built regional meshes that they don't understand. MQTT downlinking absolutely screwed up MT. You're going to get tremendous pushback here because of that alone. The PNW mesh is growing and expanding. Typically we already average ~8% channel utilization, with busy times getting up to about 17%. We don't need traffic from across the country cluttering up the airwaves with packets. If you want to connect two places with MQTT with LoRa as your last hop, go to MT. If you want mesh communication that works, stay here and add repeaters.
